text: The patient was scheduled for robotic enucleation of the leiomyoma.
We performed a forearm technique with a 10 millimeter auxiliary port.
Firstly, the pulmonary ligament was sectioned with the Maryland bipolar forceps in order to mobilize the lung and facilitate esophageal visualization.
Secondly, the mediastinal pleura was divided anteriorly to expose the anterior aspect of the esophagus.
The athigus vein was dissected and divided with an endo stapler.
The mediastinal pleura was also divided posteriorly.
A myotomy of the muscular layer of the esophagus was performed with a Maryland bipolar forceps in order to expose and visualize the tumor.
Lesions of the mucosa should be avoided during this maneuver.

The myotomy was prolonged cranially and caudally, and blunt dissection and cauterization of adhesions between the tumor and the muscular and mucosal layers was carried out in order to set free the tumor.
Several tractal sutures were placed in different points of the tumor.
Pulling of these shooters, the surgeon could mobilize the tumor and better expose points of adhesions and tension.

As in this particular case the leiomyoma was lobulated, firstly the lower and bigger part of the tumor was dissected and liberated from the esophagus with blunt dissection and cauterization with Maryland bipolar forceps in order to avoid mucosa injuries.
Once liberated from the esophagus, the specimen was inserted into an endobag.
Later, the upper and smaller part of the tumor was removed in the same manner.

After the complete removal of the tumor, the mucosa was perfectly visualized and an air bolus was introduced in the esophagus through the nasogastric tube in order to check the absence of esophageal leaks.
Suture of the myotomy was performed with a 3-0 barf V-lock suture.
BARF sutures facilitate the closure of the myotomy by making it unnecessary to knock the suture and providing a correct tension.
Finally, the nasogastric tube was removed and a 24-frame chest tube was inserted into the pleural cavity.
